summary: On the morning of September 11th, 2001, I was on duty at the park when I first heard about the plane crashing into the World Trade Center. Shortly after, reports came in about a plane crashing into the Pentagon, and the situation quickly escalated. I was deployed downtown to the National Mall with my squad to assist with security during this unprecedented emergency. The day was surreal, with the city unusually quiet and dominated by law enforcement and emergency personnel. It was a sobering and emotional experience, especially driving near the still-smoking Pentagon that evening.

I first learned about the World Trade Center when my wife paged me early that morning to tell me a plane had crashed into it. I immediately turned on the radio and TV to follow the news as the story unfolded. The crash was the initial event that signaled the severity of the attacks on September 11th. Although I was not physically there, the World Trade Center crash was the catalyst for the emergency response that involved my deployment later that day.

During the attacks on September 11th, I heard over the radio that a low-flying plane had crashed near the Pentagon, followed by reports of an explosion and smoke. Later that day, I was deployed to assist with security operations in response to the Pentagon attack. Driving home that evening, I passed near the Pentagon and saw the flames and smoke firsthand, which was a very sobering and emotional moment for me. Living less than two miles from the Pentagon made the event feel very close and personal.

On the morning of September 11th, while on duty, I heard a report from a park police officer about a low-flying plane over Arlington National Cemetery near the Pentagon. This was one of the first unusual signs that something was seriously wrong that day. Although I did not have direct duties at the cemetery, it was part of the area involved in the unfolding events and emergency response. The proximity of Arlington National Cemetery to the Pentagon made it a significant location in the early reports.

After the Pentagon attack on September 11th, I was instructed to prepare to go downtown and met with other Rangers at the George Washington Memorial Parkway, specifically at Turkey Run Park, which served as a staging area. This location was designated for assembling squads of Law Enforcement Rangers before deployment to assist with security in DC. It was an important logistical point in coordinating our response that day.

Turkey Run Park, located along the George Washington Memorial Parkway, was the staging area where I and other Rangers gathered after the Pentagon attack on September 11th. From there, squads were deployed to various locations including the National Mall and the Pentagon daycare evacuation. It was a key site for organizing our law enforcement response during the crisis.

On September 11th, after the Pentagon attack, my squad was deployed to the National Mall in Washington, DC, where I served as the squad leader. We patrolled the Mall corridor and maintained security under a state of emergency. The Mall was eerily quiet that day, with almost no civilians and only law enforcement and emergency vehicles present. I worked closely with the park police at their mobile command post near the Smithsonian Institution to coordinate our efforts.

During the response on September 11th, two Rangers from my squad were assigned to the Jefferson Memorial to work alongside park police officers. While I spent most of my time at the command post, I occasionally checked on the Rangers stationed there. The Jefferson Memorial was part of the broader National Mall area where we maintained security and monitored the situation.

The park police mobile command post was set up on the National Mall near the Smithsonian Institution during the September 11th response. I met with the park police captain there to receive assignments and coordinate our squad’s patrols. The Smithsonian area served as a central hub for law enforcement operations that day.

On September 11th, I was on duty as a Law Enforcement Ranger and closely worked with the United States Park Police during the response. I heard initial reports from their officers about the low-flying plane near Arlington Cemetery and the Pentagon explosion. Later, I coordinated with the park police captain at the mobile command post on the National Mall, serving as a liaison between my squad and the park police. Our agencies worked together to maintain security and manage the emergency response throughout the day.

During the September 11th attacks, I was informed that control of the airspace over Washington, DC, had been assumed by the United States military as part of the emergency response. While on the National Mall, I observed military aircraft flying overhead, which added to the surreal and tense atmosphere. The military’s involvement was a critical part of the heightened security measures that day.

My wife was on a detail in Lakewood, Washington, at the regional office when she paged me early on the morning of September 11th to inform me about the plane crash at the World Trade Center. Her being out of town and contacting me so early was unusual and alerted me that something serious was happening. Although I was physically in Virginia, her location in Lakewood played a role in how I first learned about the attacks.

While the transcript mentions Lakewood, Washington, where my wife was on detail, it does not specifically reference Seattle. However, Lakewood is near Seattle, and my wife’s detail there was part of the context in which I received early news about the September 11th attacks. My own activities and response were based in the Washington, DC area.

The transcript does not mention any direct involvement with Lake Roosevelt National Recreation Area during the events of September 11th. My duties and deployment were focused around the George Washington Memorial Parkway, National Mall, and Pentagon areas in the Washington, DC region. Therefore, Lake Roosevelt was not part of my experience on that day.
